items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1028 / RY 14 (1618-1619 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur al-Din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (Nur-ud-din Jahangir Shah, [son of] Akbar Shah).
Reverse: Persian legend indicating the Ilahi month 'Farwardin' (the 1st month), the regnal year '14', and the mint name 'Patna' at the bottom.

This is an 'Elahi' type coin where the month of issue is specified using the Persian solar calendar. The coin is housed in a standard 2x2 cardboard holder with correct historical attributions. The first month of the Elahi calendar is Farwardin.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1027 / 1617 AD (Regnal Year 13). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Light of the Faith Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor and Fighter for the Faith).
Reverse: Persian legend indicating the Ilahi month 'Ardibihisht' (the 2nd month), the Hijri year '1027', and the mint name 'Patna' at the bottom.

This is a silver rupee from the Mughal Empire under Jahangir. It features the Ilahi month system which was a solar calendar used during the reigns of Akbar and Jahangir. The second month of the Elahi calendar is Ardibihisht.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1025 / Regnal Year 11 (1616 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur al-Din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (Nuruddin Jahangir Shah, son of Akbar Shah)
Reverse: Persian legend containing the Ilahi month 'Khordad' (3rd month), the mint name 'Patna', and the regnal year 11.

This is an 'Elahi' type silver rupee. Unlike typical Islamic coins that primarily use the Hijri calendar, this series uses the solar Ilahi months introduced by Akbar. The third month of the Elahi calendar is Khordad.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1024 / AD 1615 (Regnal Year 10). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: "Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi" (Light of the Religion Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name "Patna", the Elahi month "Tir" (the 4th month), and the Hijri year "1024".

This is an 'Elahi' type Rupee issued during the middle of Jahangir's reign. These coins are noted for using the months of the Persian solar calendar (Ilahi calendar) instead of just the Hijri date. The fourth month of the Elahi calendar is Tir.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1024 / 1615 AD (Regnal Year 10). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (Nur-ud-din Jahangir, King, [son of] Akbar, King).
Reverse: Persian legend identifying the mint 'Patna' at the bottom, the Elahi month 'Murdad' (5th month) in the middle, and the Hijri year '1024' often with the regnal year '10'.

This coin is a silver Rupee of the Elahi type, which continued the calendar system established by Akbar. The use of specific solar months (Murdad in this case) on coinage is a characteristic feature of Jahangir's early to mid-reign issues. The fifth month of the Elahi calendar is Murdad.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1027 / 1617 AD. Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (The light of religion, Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor and Fighter for the Faith).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the Elahi month (Shahrewar, 6th month), the regnal year, and the mint name 'Patna' at the bottom.
This coin is an Elahi type Rupee, which uses the Persian solar calendar months instead of the standard Hijri lunar months. The sixth month of the Elahi calendar is Shahrewar.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1022 / RY 8 (1613 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur al-Din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (The light of the faith Jahangir, King, son of Akbar, King).
Reverse: Persian legend featuring the Elahi month 'Mehr' (the 7th month), the regnal year '8', and the mint name 'Zarb Patna' (Struck at Patna) at the bottom.

This is a silver rupee of the 'Elahi' type. The handwritten note on the holder identifies it as the 7th month, which is Mehr. The Persian calendar system introduced by Akbar was maintained by Jahangir.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1023 / 1614 AD (Regnal Year 9, Month: Aban). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur al-din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (Light of the Faith, Jahangir Shah, [son of] Akbar Shah).
Reverse: Persian legend including the mint name 'Patna', the Elahi month 'Aban' (the 8th month of the Persian solar calendar used by Mughals), and the Hijri date '1023'.

This is a popular 'Elahi' month type silver rupee issued by Jahangir. The eighth month of the Elahi calendar is Aban. The handwritten notes on the holder accurately identify the ruler, mint, and month.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1027 / RY 12 (c. 1617-1618 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Light of the Religion, Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend including the Elahi month 'Azar' (9th month), the Regnal Year (RY), and the mint name 'Patna'.

This is an Elahi type silver rupee characterized by the inclusion of the Persian solar month name. The ninth month of the Elahi calendar is Azar. Patna mint coins from this period are highly collectible for their calligraphy.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1030 / 1620 AD (Regnal Year 15/16). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Jahangir Shah Akbar Shah' (Light of the Faith Jahangir, King, [son of] Akbar, King).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Patna' and the Elahi month 'Dey' (the 10th month of the Persian solar calendar).

This is an 'Elahi' type rupee, which uses the divine calendar months introduced by Akbar. The tenth month of the Elahi calendar is Dey. These coins are popular among collectors for their high-relief calligraphy.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1029 (c. 1619-1620 AD); Ilahi month Bahman (11th month). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Light of the Religion, Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior of the Faith).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Patna' at the bottom, the Hijri year '1029', and the Elahi month 'Bahman' (indicated as the 11th month on the flip).
A well-preserved silver rupee from the Patna mint. Jahangir's coinage continued the 'Elahi' calendar system, where coins were issued with specific month names. The eleventh month is Bahman.
items.period: AH 1029 (c. 1619-1620 AD); Ilahi month Bahman (11th month)

metadata.year_or_period: AH 1029 (c. 1619-1620 AD); Ilahi month Bahman (11th month)

items.title: Silver Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1029 (approx. 1619-1620 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Light of the Faith Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Patna', the Elahi month 'Isfandarmuz' (indicated as the 12th month), and the Hijri year '1029'.

This coin belongs to the popular 'Elahi' month series of Jahangir. The twelfth month of the Elahi calendar is Isfandarmuz. Patna was a significant provincial mint in the Mughal Empire during this period.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037-1068 / 1628-1658 AD. Mint: Not fully legible (Typical Mughal Mint). Catalog: Mughal Empire - Standard Silver Coinage.
notes: Obverse: The Kalima (Islamic profession of faith) in a central square; names and attributes of the four Rightly Guided Caliphs in the margins.
Reverse: Full name and title of the Emperor: 'Shihab-ud-din Muhammad Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi'; Mint name and regnal year often located at the bottom.
A well-preserved specimen of the 'Square Area' type, which was standard for much of Shah Jahan's reign. The holder indicates the full span of his reign.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037-1068. Mint: Patna. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Patna Mint Series.
notes: Obverse: Kalima Shahada within a square border, names of the four companions of the Prophet in the margins.
Reverse: Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi and Mint name 'Patna' clearly visible at the bottom.
Patna mint coins are popular among collectors of provincial Mughal issues. The strike is centered.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037-1068. Mint: Patna. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Patna Mint Series.
notes: Obverse: Square area type with Kalima; margins contain 'bi-sidq Abi Bakr', 'wa 'adl 'Umar', 'ba-hoya 'Uthman', 'wa 'ilm 'Ali'.
Reverse: Royal title of Shah Jahan; Mint name 'Patna' in the lower segment.
Similar to coin 2, representing the steady output of the Patna mint during the mid-17th century.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1042 / Regnal Year 5 / Month Aban. Mint: Patna. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Ilahi Month Series.
notes: Obverse: Kalima in square, Hijri date 1042 clearly visible.
Reverse: Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi, Regnal Year 5, Persian month 'Aban', and Mint Patna.
Coins featuring the Persian Ilahi months are highly collectible. The month 'Aban' corresponds to October-November.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: Regnal Year 13. Mint: Patna. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Patna Mint.
notes: Obverse: Standard Kalima in square with names of Caliphs in margins.
Reverse: Emperor's name and title, Regnal Year 13, and Patna mint name.
Consistent with the high-quality silver coinage produced at the Patna mint during Shah Jahan's peak years.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037 / 1628 AD (Accession Year). Mint: Akbarabad (Agra). Catalog: Mughal Empire - Capital Mint Series.
notes: Obverse: Kalima in square, date AH 1037 visible. Excellent calligraphy.
Reverse: Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi, Mint 'Akbarabad' at the bottom. The first year of his reign.
Akbarabad was the primary capital of the empire. Accession year coins from the capital are always in high demand.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037 / 1628 AD. Mint: Surat. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Surat Mint Series.
notes: Obverse: Kalima in a lozenge or four-petaled flower shape, surrounded by Caliphs' names.
Reverse: Shah Jahan's titles with AH 1037 and Mint name 'Surat'.
Surat was the busiest port and mint of the Mughal Empire. This design is slightly more ornate than the standard square type.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1038 / 1629 AD. Mint: Multan. Catalog: Mughal Empire - Multan Mint.
notes: Obverse: Kalima in square with Hijri date 1038.
Reverse: Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi with Multan mint mark.
Multan was a strategic frontier mint. Coins from this mint are generally scarcer than those of Surat or Agra.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I. Period: AH 1037-1068. Mint: Qandhar (Kandahar). Catalog: Mughal Empire - Frontier Mints.
notes: Obverse: Kalima Shahada in square area.
Reverse: Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi, with Mint name 'Qandhar' visible.
Rare. Control of Qandhar was contested between the Mughals and the Safavids. This mint is highly prized for its historical significance and rarity.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1030 / 1620 AD. Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM #145.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels) within a floral scrollwork border.
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Patna', the Hijri date '1030', and potentially an Elahi month, set against a background of delicate floral scrolls.

This coin is a silver rupee of the 'Elahi' type, which uses the Persian solar calendar months. The calligraphy and the intricate floral ornamentation are hallmarks of Jahangir's coinage, often considered the most artistic of the Mughal period. The holder correctly identifies the ruler, mint, and date.

items.title: Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: 1014-1020 AH (1605-1612 AD). Mint: Ahmadnagar. Catalog: Krause KM# 141, 145 series.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi'. The middle line clearly shows 'Jahangir'.
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Zarb Ahmadnagar' and the Hijri date/Regnal Year (not fully visible but attributed by the holder).

The coin is a silver rupee of Jahangir from the Ahmadnagar mint in the Deccan region. While the holder mentions 'Salim' (his birth name), the coin itself bears his imperial title 'Jahangir'. Ahmadnagar was a strategic mint town during the Mughal expansion into southern India.

items.title: Rupee - Mughal Emperor Jahangir
items.description: Rupee issued by Mughal Emperor Jahangir. Period: AH 1014-1037 / 1605-1627 AD. Mint: Ahmadnagar. Catalog: KM# 142.1.
notes: Obverse: Features the Kalima (Islamic declaration of faith): 'La illaha ill Allah Muhammadur Rasul Allah' within a central field.
Reverse: Persian legend containing the Emperor's name and title: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' with the mint name 'Ahmadnagar' at the bottom.

The handwritten label identifies this as a 'Kalma Variety' (Kalima type) from the Ahmadnagar mint. The reference to 'Salim' on the holder refers to Jahangir's pre-regnal name. These silver rupees from Ahmadnagar are well-regarded by collectors of Mughal Deccan coinage.

items.title: Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1021 / 1612 AD. Mint: Qandahar (Quandhar). Catalog: KM #145.13 (Nur-ud-din type).
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur al-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (The Light of the Faith, Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Qandahar' and the Hijri year '1021'. Usually includes a couplet or standard mint formula.

Qandahar was a major strategic mint town on the border of the Mughal and Safavid empires. Its coins are highly collectible because of the constant geopolitical shifts in the region during the 17th century. The attribution on the flip matches the visible Persian script and typical Jahangir coinage of this period.

items.title: Rupee - Jahangir (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahangir (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1022 / AD 1613 (Regnal Year 8). Mint: Tatta (Thatta, present-day Pakistan). Catalog: KM# 141.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir Badshah Ghazi' (Light of the Faith, Muhammad Jahangir, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Tatta' at the bottom, the AH date 1022, and the Regnal Year (RY) 8.

The coin features clear Persian calligraphy typical of the Tatta mint under Jahangir. The AH date 1022 is clearly visible at the bottom of the reverse. Tatta was a prolific mint for silver coinage in the Sindh province during the Mughal period. The coin appears to be in a standard 2x2 cardboard flip with identification notes that match the visible numismatic data.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: Reign: 1628-1658 AD (approx. 1037-1068 AH). Mint: Lahore. Catalog: KM# 235 (Standard Silver Rupee series).
notes: Obverse: The Kalima (Islamic Creed): 'La ilaha illa Allah Muhammadur Rasulullah' usually within a central circle or quatrefoil, surrounded by the names of the four Caliphs.
Reverse: Full titles of the Emperor: 'Shihab al-Din Muhammad Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi', along with the mint name (Lahore) and the Regnal Year (RY).
The identification is supported by the contemporary numismatic notes on the holder. Shah Jahan's rupees from the Lahore mint are common but highly sought after for their calligraphic beauty.

items.title: Gold Mohur - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Gold Mohur issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1061 (Regnal Year 23 / 1650 AD). Mint: Daulatabad. Catalog: KM# 260.10 (Standard Catalog of World Coins).
notes: Obverse: The Kalima inscribed inside a square frame: 'La ilaha illa Allah, Muhammadur Rasulullah'. The names of the four orthodox Caliphs occupy the segments outside the square.
Reverse: The Emperor's title 'Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi' inside a square frame. The mint name 'Daulatabad' and the regnal year '23' are visible in the margins.
High-quality 'Square Area' type gold Mohur. The Daulatabad mint was a major strategic site in the Deccan. AH 1061 matches the 23rd regnal year of Shah Jahan perfectly.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1037-1068 / 1628-1658 AD. Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 224.16.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend containing the ruler's title: 'Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi' (Shah Jahan, Emperor and Warrior for the Faith).
Reverse: Islamic Kalima in the center or within a geometric design, surrounded by the names of the four Rashidun Caliphs, with the mint name 'Patna' at the bottom.
Housed in a professional flip with handwritten identification. Shah Jahan's coinage from Patna is well-regarded for its high-quality calligraphy and consistent weight.

items.title: Nisar (Silver ceremonial coin) - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Nisar (Silver ceremonial coin) issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: 1039 AH / 1629 AD. Mint: Likely Agra or Ahmedabad. Catalog: KM #220 (Mughal Empire - Shah Jahan Nisar series).
notes: Obverse: Persian legend containing the Emperor's name and title: 'Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi'
Reverse: Persian legend indicating the mint name and the AH date 1039
A 'Nisar' is a legal tender ceremonial coin minted for scattering during royal processions. Square-shaped silver nisars of Shah Jahan are highly sought after by collectors.

metadata.series_or_catalog: KM #220 (Mughal Empire - Shah Jahan Nisar series)

items.title: Dam - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Dam issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1039 / 1629 AD. Mint: Likely Surat or Ahmedabad. Catalog: Krause KM# 124.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend with the Emperor's titles: 'Shihab-ud-din Muhammad Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi' (partially visible)
Reverse: Persian inscription typically denoting the mint and the Hijri date AH 1039
Identified by collector's handwriting on the flip. These copper dams were thick, heavy currency pieces used in daily commerce early in Shah Jahan's reign.

items.title: 1/16 Rupee (One Anna) - Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1/16 Rupee (One Anna) issued by Shah Jahan I (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1039 (1629 AD). Mint: Likely Surat or Ahmedabad. Catalog: KM# 210 series (Mughal Coinage).
notes: Obverse: Persian legend containing the name and titles of the emperor: 'Shah Jahan Badshah Ghazi'.
Reverse: The Islamic Kalima: 'La ilaha illa Allah Muhammadur Rasulullah'. On square types, names of the four Rashidun Caliphs often appear in the corners.
Small silver fractional coin from early reign. Square coinage was characteristic of certain mints like Surat and Ahmedabad during his first few regnal years.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1079 (c. 1668-1669 AD). Mint: Patna (Note: labeled Azimabad, but Azimabad name was used later). Catalog: KM# 300 series.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with AH 1079.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah Julus Zarb Patna'.
Historical label discrepancy: The holder says Azimabad, but that name was adopted in 1704. A coin dated AH 1079 would typically be minted as 'Patna'. The coin appears dark/toned.
items.region: Patna (Note: labeled Azimabad, but Azimabad name was used later)

metadata.mint_or_place: Patna (Note: labeled Azimabad, but Azimabad name was used later)

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb (Alamgir I)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb (Alamgir I). Period: 1658-1707 AD (AH 1068-1118). Mint: Akbarabad (Agra). Catalog: KM# 300.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu meh-r munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Akbarabad' at the bottom, and 'Manus Maimanat Sanah Julus'.
Standard silver rupee from the Akbarabad mint. Calligraphy is characteristic of Aurangzeb's coinage.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1116 / 1704 AD. Mint: Etawa (Itawa). Catalog: KM# 300.28.
notes: Obverse: Standard 'Badr Munir' couplet in Persian with Emperor's name.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah [RY] Julus Zarb Itawa'.
Reverse shows the mint name 'Itawa' clearly at the bottom. Date AH 1116 corresponds to later reign.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1118 / Regnal Year 51 (c. 1706-1707 AD). Mint: Khambayat (Cambay). Catalog: KM# 300.51.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat [51] Zarb Khambayat'.
Minted during the final regnal year. Khambayat was a major port mint in Gujarat.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: 1658-1707 AD. Mint: Lakhnau (Lucknow). Catalog: KM# 300.54.
notes: Obverse: Persian poetic legend with the Emperor's name.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Lakhnau, Sanah Manus Maimanat'.
Lucknow was a prominent mint. The mint name is clearly visible at the bottom of the reverse.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1084 / 1673 AD. Mint: Akbarnagar (Rajmahal). Catalog: KM# 300.7.
notes: Obverse: Standard 'Badr-e-Munir' couplet legend.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Manus Sanah [RY] Zarb Akbarnagar'.
Akbarnagar (modern Rajmahal) was a primary mint in Bengal province.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1100 (c. 1688-1689 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 300.12.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend with date AH 1100.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat Julus' with mint name 'Azimabad'.
Holder mentions 1692 AD, though AH 1100 is 1688-89. Azimabad name refers to Patna.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1118 / 1706 AD. Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 300.15.
notes: Obverse: Persian poetic couplet with AH 1118.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat [RY] Zarb Azimabad'.
From the very end of Aurangzeb's reign. Patna was officially renamed Azimabad in 1704.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1100 (c. 1688-1689 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 300.71.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend featuring the Hijri date 1100.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah [RY] Zarb Patna'.
Silver rupee from Patna mint. Dark toning visible on the surface.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1109 (1697-1698 AD). Mint: Patna. Catalog: KM# 300.71.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu mihr munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' (Struck coin in the world like the shining sun, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Patna' at the bottom, 'Manus Maimanat Julus' (Associated with prosperity of the reign), and includes the regnal year.
The coin flip identifies the ruler as Aurangzeb and the mint as Patna with the date AH 1109. Patna was an important administrative and trade center during the Mughal era.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: 1658-1707 AD. Mint: Banaras (Muhammadabad Banaras). Catalog: KM# 300.24.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' (Struck coin in the world like the shining moon, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir).
Reverse: Persian legend 'Zarb Muhammadabad Banaras' along with the Regnal Year (RY) and the 'Manus Maimanat' formula.
The dark, brownish-black appearance suggests heavy oxidation or 'cabinet toning' of the silver surface. Coins from the Banaras mint are highly sought after.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1107 (1695-1696 AD). Mint: Bijapur (Dar-ul-Zafar). Catalog: KM# 300.23.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Julus Sanah [RY] Zarb Dar-ul-Zafar Bijapur'.
The epithet 'Dar-ul-Zafar' (Abode of Victory) was used for Bijapur after its conquest in 1686. Silver rupees from this mint are historically significant for the Deccan campaigns.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: 1687-1707 AD. Mint: Golkunda. Catalog: KM# 300.28.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu mihr-e-munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat manus, sanah [RY] julus, zarb Golkunda'.
Minted after the fall of the Golkunda Sultanate in 1687. The coin reflects the consolidation of Mughal power in the South.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1107 (1695 AD). Mint: Junagarh. Catalog: KM# 300.43.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Junagarh' (Minted at Junagarh) with the regnal year.
The holder identifies this as a Junagarh silver rupee. The dark toning on the silver is typical of coins stored in older flips.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1108 / RY 40 (1696-1697 AD). Mint: Lahore (Dar-ul-Sultanat). Catalog: KM# 300.52.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with AH date 1108.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat sanah 40 julus zarb Lahore'.
Lahore was a major mint center designated as 'Dar-ul-Sultanat'. The 'Badr Munir' couplet is characteristic of Aurangzeb's silver coinage.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1100 (1689-1690 AD). Mint: Multan. Catalog: KM# 300.56.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr-e-munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with AH date 1100.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat sanah julus zarb Multan'.
A high-quality specimen showing excellent detail. Multan was a key mint city on the trade routes to the west.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: 1658-1707 AD. Mint: Muazzamabad (Gorakhpur). Catalog: KM# 300.60.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu mihr munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend 'Manus maimanat sanat [RY] zarb Muazzamabad'.
Muazzamabad was the honorific name for Gorakhpur. Coins from this mint are scarcer and highly valued by collectors.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1110 (1698 AD). Mint: Shahjahanabad (Delhi). Catalog: KM# 300.93.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr-e-munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with date 1110.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Masus Zarb Shahjahanabad'.
Shahjahanabad refers to Mughal Delhi. This is a classic example of the coinage of the capital.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1115 (1703-1704 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 300.86.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with date 1115.
Reverse: Persian legend 'Zarb Surat Sanat [RY] Julus Manus Maimanat'.
Surat was the busiest port in the empire and its mint produced a vast quantity of silver rupees.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1114 (1702 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 300.86.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu mihr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with date 1114.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat manus zarb Surat sanah julus [RY]'.
Another Surat rupee from the late reign of Aurangzeb, showing consistent weight and purity.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: 1658-1707 AD. Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 300.86.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu badr munir Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat sanah [RY] julus zarb Surat'.
A well-preserved silver rupee from the prolific Surat mint.

items.title: Rupee - Aurangzeb Alamgir
items.description: Rupee issued by Aurangzeb Alamgir. Period: AH 1074 (1663-1664 AD). Mint: No Mint Mark Visible. Catalog: KM# 300.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad dar jahan chu mihr munir, Shah Aurangzeb Alamgir' with date 1074.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat sanah [RY] julus'. Mint name is off-flan.
Early reign rupee with clear Hijri date 1074. The mint name is not visible on the flan.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1119 / RY Ahad (1707 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 347.19.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Shah Alam Bahadur).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah Julus Ahad Zarb Azimabad' (Struck at Azimabad in the Year One of the reign associated with prosperity).

The coin belongs to the first year (Ahad) of Shah Alam I's reign, immediately following the death of Aurangzeb. Azimabad was a significant Mughal mint in Bihar. The handwriting on the coin flip provides accurate metadata that matches the visible numismatic features of the coin.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1120 (1708 AD), Regnal Year 2. Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM #347.4.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Shah Ghazi' with AH date 1120 at the bottom.
Reverse: Persian legend containing the mint name 'Azimabad' and the regnal year 'Julus 2' (as indicated on the holder).

Shah Alam I, the eldest son of Aurangzeb, reigned for a brief period (1707-1712). Coins from the Azimabad mint are well-regarded for their calligraphy. This specimen shows clear legends and the date is easily readable.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1120 / RY 3 (1708-1709 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: Krause KM# 348.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Shah Alam Bahadur). The Hijri date '1120' is visible at the bottom.

Reverse: Though not fully visible, the holder identifies it as the reverse side with legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Julus Zarb Azimabad' (Struck at Azimabad in the 3rd year of the associated prosperous reign).

Shah Alam I was the seventh Mughal Emperor of India, ruling from 1707 to 1712. Azimabad (modern Patna) was an important administrative and minting center in the Bengal Subah. The coin is well-preserved within a numismatic flip with accurate collector annotations.

items.title: Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I), Mughal Emperor
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I), Mughal Emperor. Period: AH 1121 / 1710 AD (Regnal Year 4). Mint: Azimabad (Modern-day Patna, Bihar). Catalog: KM# 347.4.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Shah Alam), with Hijri date 1121 partially visible.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 4 Julus Zarb Azimabad' (Struck at Azimabad in the 4th year of his reign associated with prosperity).

A well-preserved silver rupee of the Mughal Empire. Shah Alam I was the successor to Aurangzeb. Azimabad, the mint name, refers to the city of Patna. The coin features the 'Mubarak' (Auspicious) couplet. The details on the flip accurately describe the coin shown.

metadata.ruler_or_issuer: Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I), Mughal Emperor

items.title: 1 Rupee - Shah Alam I (also known as Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (also known as Bahadur Shah I). Period: 1709 AD (AH 1121, Regnal Year 3). Mint: Khujistabad (Aurangabad). Catalog: Krause KM# 348.18.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (The auspicious coin of Shah Alam Bahadur, the Victorious Emperor). The AH date 1121 is typically found here.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Julus Zarb Khujistabad' (Associated with prosperity, struck at Khujistabad in the 3rd year of his reign).

Khujistabad was the honorific name used for the Aurangabad mint during the Mughal era. This coin is a typical hammered silver rupee of the late Mughal period. The details on the flip-holder accurately describe the coin visible.

metadata.ruler_or_issuer: Shah Alam I (also known as Bahadur Shah I)

items.title: Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1122 / 1710 AD (Regnal Year 4 or 5). Mint: Itawa (Etawah). Catalog: KM# 348.17.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of Emperor Shah Alam Bahadur, the Victorious King). The Hijri date 1122 is typically found on this side.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat [RY] Zarb Itawa' (Struck at Itawa in the [RY] year of the reign of tranquil prosperity). The mint name 'Itawa' is visible at the bottom.

Shah Alam I, also known as Bahadur Shah I, was the son of Aurangzeb and reigned from 1707 to 1712 AD. The Itawa mint, located in present-day Uttar Pradesh, was a significant provincial mint during the Mughal era. The identification on the coin holder matches the visible numismatic evidence.

items.title: Rupee - Mughal Emperor Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Rupee issued by Mughal Emperor Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1119 (1707 AD), Regnal Year 1 (Ahd). Mint: Karimabad. Catalog: Krause KM# 347.11.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of Shah Alam, Emperor and fighter against the infidels). The AH date 1119 is visible at the top.
Reverse: Standard Mughal formula: 'Manus Maimanat Manus Zarb Karimabad Sanah Ahd Jalus' (Associated with prosperity, struck at Karimabad in the first year of the accession).

This coin marks the first year of the reign of Shah Alam I (the successor to Aurangzeb). The Karimabad mint was located in Bengal and is highly collectible. The attribution on the cardboard holder matches the visible legends on the coin.

metadata.ruler_or_issuer: Mughal Emperor Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)

items.title: Silver Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1121 / RY 3 (1709 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM #348.34.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Shah Alam Bahadur), with AH date 1121.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Surat Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Julus' (Struck at Surat in the 3rd year of the prosperous reign).

Surat was one of the most prolific mints of the Mughal Empire. This specimen features the 'Julus' formula typical of the period. The regnal year (RY 3) corresponds with the Hijri date 1121 shown on the holder. The coin appears to be authentic and consistent with the descriptions on the flip.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Shah Alam I (Bahadur Shah I). Period: AH 1124 / 1712 AD (Regnal Year 6). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 348.13.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Shah Alam Bahadur Badshah Ghazi' (Auspicious coin of Emperor Shah Alam, the Valiant, the Warrior of the Faith).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Surat' (Minted at Surat) at the bottom, with the formula 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 6' (In the 6th year of the reign associated with prosperity).

The image shows the reverse of the coin. Shah Alam I was the son of Aurangzeb and his reign was relatively short (1707–1712). Coins from the Surat mint are quite common for this period, but specimens with clear dates and mint names are desirable. The date AH 1124 corresponds to his final year of rule.

items.title: Rupee - Jahandar Shah
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahandar Shah. Period: AH 1124 / 1712 AD (Regnal Year 1). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 363.22.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Zad bar zad chun mehr-i-sahib-i-qiran / Jahandar Shah, padshah-i-jahan' (Struck like the sun of the Lord of the Conjunctions, Jahandar Shah, Emperor of the World).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah Ahad Zarb Surat' (Associated with prosperity, in the year one of the reign, struck at Surat).

Jahandar Shah's coins are scarce due to his short reign of approximately 11 months. The Surat mint issues are well-regarded for their calligraphy. This coin is housed in a standard 2x2 collector's holder.

items.title: Rupee - Jahandar Shah
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahandar Shah. Period: AH 1124 / 1712 AD (Regnal Year 1). Mint: Shahjahanabad (Delhi). Catalog: KM# 363.21.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Zad bar khurshid-o-mah chun mah-e-nau, sikka-e-daulat-o-iqbal dar jahan Jahandar Shah' (Struck like the sun and the moon, the coin of fortune and prosperity in the world, Jahandar Shah).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Shahjahanabad Sanat Ahad Julus-e-Maimanat-e-Manus' (Struck at Shahjahanabad in the first year of his reign of prosperous association).
The Shahjahanabad (Delhi) mint was the primary imperial mint. Coins from the capital mint are historically significant. This specimen clearly shows the AH date 1124.

items.title: Rupee - Jahandar Shah
items.description: Rupee issued by Jahandar Shah. Period: AH 1124 / 1712 AD (Regnal Year 1). Mint: Likely Shahjahanabad or Surat. Catalog: KM# 363.
notes: Obverse: Persian couplet typically including the name of Jahandar Shah and references to the sun and moon or the Lord of Conjunctions.
Reverse: Persian legend with the 'Manus Maimanat' formula, the regnal year 'Ahad' (1), and the mint name.
Identification is supported by the handwritten label on the 2x2 holder. Though the image is slightly less clear than the others, it belongs to the same set of Jahandar Shah silver issues.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: c. 1713 AD (Regnal Year 1 / AH 1124-1125). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 377.10.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend containing the name and titles of the Emperor: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haq bar haft kishwar, Shah Farrukhsiyar Badshah-i-bahr-o-bar' (The monarch of the sea and land, Shah Farrukhsiyar, struck coin in the seven climes by the grace of the true God).

Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Azimabad' (Struck at Azimabad) at the bottom; 'Sanah Jalaus' (In the year of the accession) with the regnal year 1, and the 'Manus Maimanat' formula (Associated with prosperity).

The coin is housed in a flip where the owner has correctly identified the ruler as Farrukhsiyar and the mint as Azimabad (modern-day Patna). Farrukhsiyar's coinage is known for its elegant calligraphy. Azimabad was an important provincial mint in the Bihar region during the later Mughal period.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1125, Regnal Year 2 (c. 1714 AD). Mint: Azimabad (modern-day Patna). Catalog: KM# 377.7.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haqq bar sim o zar, Badshah-i-bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin on silver and gold by the grace of Truth, the monarch of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 2 Zarb Azimabad' (Associated with prosperity, year 2, struck at Azimabad).

The coin clearly shows the 'Manus Maimanat' formula and the mint name Azimabad. The handwriting on the holder accurately identifies the ruler, mint, and dates. Farrukhsiyar's coinage from Azimabad is well-documented and historically significant for the Bihar region during the later Mughal period.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1129 / RY 5 (c. 1717 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 377.7.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend (likely): 'Sikka zad az fazl-e-haq bar haft kishwar, Shahanshah-e-Bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of God on the seven climes, King of kings of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar). AH date 1129 is usually present.

Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Azimabad sanah 5 julus maimanat manus' (Struck at Azimabad in the 5th year of the prosperous reign). The regnal year '5' and the mint name 'Azimabad' are clearly visible.

The coin image shows the reverse side of a Mughal silver rupee. Farrukhsiyar is historically significant for granting the 'Farman' of 1717 to the British East India Company, which waived customs duties in Bengal. Azimabad was a major Mughal mint located in present-day Patna, Bihar.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: 1127 AH / Regnal Year 4 (c. 1715 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 377.10.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Zad Az Fazl-e-Haq Bar Do Alam, Badshah-e-Bahr-o-Bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of Truth on the two worlds, king of the sea and land, Farrukhsiyar).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 4 Zarb Azimabad' (Associated with prosperity, year 4, struck at Azimabad).

The coin features clear Persian calligraphy typical of the later Mughal period. Azimabad (modern Patna) was a significant provincial capital and mint. The identification on the collector's flip is consistent with the visible portions of the coin's legend.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1130 / RY 7 (approx. 1717-1718 AD). Mint: Akbarabad (Agra), Mustaqir-ul-Khilafat. Catalog: KM# 377.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haq bar haft kishwar, Shahanshah-i-bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of God on the seven climes, the Emperor of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar). Note the AH date 1130 is usually at the bottom or near the top.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat 7 Julus Zarb Akbarabad' (Struck at Akbarabad in the 7th year of his prosperous reign).

The coin holder contains a misspelling ('Fakrukhsiyar'). Farrukhsiyar reigned from 1713 to 1719 AD. Akbarabad was a premier mint for the Mughals. The coin is a standard hammered silver rupee with typical off-center striking characteristic of the period.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar. Period: 1713-1719 AD (AH 1124-1131). Mint: Arcot (Arkat). Catalog: KM# 377 (Standard Catalog of World Coins).
notes: Obverse: Persian legend including the name of the emperor: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-Haq bar haft kishwar, Shah-i-behr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of God on the seven climes, king of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat julus zarb Arkat' (Associated with prosperity of the reign, struck at Arcot), typically including the Regnal Year (RY).

The coin exhibits several 'shroff marks' (small punch marks), which were used by money changers to authenticate the silver purity. The Arcot mint was a prolific mint in Southern India, and while originally a Mughal mint, its name was later used by the Nawabs of Arcot and the British East India Company to strike similar-style coinage.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1124 (1713 AD). Mint: Itawa (Etawa). Catalog: Krause KM# 377.24.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haq bar haft kishwar, Shahanshah-i-bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of God on the seven climes, Emperor of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar). The Hijri year 1124 is visible at the bottom left.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat julus zarb Itawa' (Associated with the prosperity of the accession, struck at Itawa).

Farrukhsiyar ruled from 1713 to 1719. AH 1124 was his accession year. The coin shows the 'seven climes' couplet which was standard for his silver rupees. Itawa was a major mint in present-day Uttar Pradesh. The handwritten label on the coin holder provides accurate metadata.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar, Mughal Emperor
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar, Mughal Emperor. Period: AH 1126 (1715 AD), Regnal Year 3. Mint: Murshidabad. Catalog: KM #377.49.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-e-haq bar haft kishwar, Shah-e-Farrukhsiyar' (The emperor Farrukhsiyar struck coin by the grace of God on the seven climes). The AH date 1126 is often seen on this type.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Manus Zarb Murshidabad Julus Sanah 3' (Struck at Murshidabad in the 3rd year of the prosperous reign).

Farrukhsiyar's coinage from Murshidabad is historically significant as Murshidabad was the seat of the Nawabs of Bengal. This specimen shows a clear mint name at the bottom of the reverse. The holder has a minor spelling error for the ruler's name.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1124 (Accession year) / 1713 AD. Mint: Shahjahanabad (Delhi). Catalog: KM# 377.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haq bar haft kishwar, Shahanshah-i-bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of Truth on the seven climes, the King of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat mahrusat' with mint name 'Dar-ul-Khilafat Shahjahanabad' and regnal year (Ahad/Year 1).

The coin flip contains a misspelling ('fakkushiyar' for Farrukhsiyar). This is a standard imperial issue from the Delhi mint. Farrukhsiyar's reign (1713-1719) was marked by the influence of the Sayyid Brothers and the granting of the 'Golden Farman' to the East India Company.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1125 (c. 1713-1714 AD). Mint: Shahjahanabad (Delhi). Catalog: KM# 377.55.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-e-haq bar do alam, Badshah bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (Struck coin by the grace of Truth on the two worlds, king of sea and land, Farrukhsiyar). The AH date 1125 is visible.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Manus Zarb Shahjahanabad' with regnal year (likely RY 2 or 3 based on the AH date).

Features the popular 'Bahr-o-Bar' poetic couplet. Shahjahanabad (modern-day Delhi) was the primary imperial mint of the Mughal Empire at this time. The coin appears well-preserved in a labeled numismatic holder.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Emperor Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Empire)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Emperor Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Empire). Period: AH 1128 / Regnal Year 4 (c. 1715-1716 AD). Mint: Sirhind (Sahrind). Catalog: KM# 377.58.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haqq bar sim o zar, Badshah-i-bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar' (The emperor of the sea and land, Farrukhsiyar, struck coin on silver and gold by the grace of the Truth).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 4 Zarb Sahrind' (Associated with prosperity, in the 4th year of the reign, struck at Sirhind).

Farrukhsiyar (reigned 1713–1719) is historically significant for granting the Farman to the British East India Company in 1717. The coin follows the standard Mughal weight and purity. The identification on the coin flip has some spelling errors ('fakkukhsiyar' and 'Shahind' for Sirhind) but correctly identifies the date and ruler. The image shows the reverse side of the coin centered in the holder.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar. Period: AH 1124 / RY Ahd (1). Mint: Azimabad. Catalog: KM# 377.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad az fazl-i-haq bar haft kishwar, Shah-en-shah bahr-o-bar Farrukhsiyar'. AH date 1124 is partially visible.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus maimanat manus, sanah ahad julus, zarb Azimabad'.
First regnal year issue. A classic Mughal silver rupee of the 'Bar haft kishwar' type.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar. Period: AH 1125 / RY 2. Mint: Azimabad. Catalog: KM# 377.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend with AH 1125 date.
Reverse: Persian legend with RY 2 and mint name Azimabad.
Well-centered strike showing clear date and regnal year.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar
items.description: Rupee issued by Farrukhsiyar. Period: AH 1129 / RY 5. Mint: Azimabad. Catalog: KM# 377.6.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend with AH 1129 date.
Reverse: Persian legend with RY 5 and mint name Azimabad. Features distinct shroff marks.
Excellent condition with significant luster remaining despite the banker's marks.

items.title: Rupee - Farrukhsiyar

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1147 / RY 17 (c. 1734 AD). Mint: Kora (Kora Jahanabad). Catalog: Krause KM# 436.39.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: Sikka Mubarak Sahib-i-qiran Sani Muhammad Shah Badshah Ghazi (The auspicious coin of the second Lord of the Conjunctions, Muhammad Shah, Emperor and Fighter against Infidels). Note: This side is not visible in the photo but is standard for this issue.
Reverse: Persian legend: Zarb Kora Sanat 17 Julus Manus Maimanat (Struck at Kora in the 17th year of his reign of prosperous association).

The coin is a silver Rupee of the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah, specifically attributed to the Kora mint (Kora Jahanabad). The regnal year (RY) 17 is visible on the reverse. The attribution on the coin holder matches the visible numismatic features of the coin.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1149 / RY 18 (c. 1736-1737 AD). Mint: Murshidabad. Catalog: KM# 436.46.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Sahib-i-qiran sani Muhammad Shah Badshah Ghazi' (The auspicious coin of the second Lord of the Conjunctions, Muhammad Shah, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Murshidabad Sanah 18 Julus Manus Maimanat' (Struck at Murshidabad in the 18th year of the reign associated with prosperity).

This is a silver rupee from the reign of Muhammad Shah 'Rangeela'. Murshidabad was a prolific mint under the Nawabs of Bengal who acknowledged Mughal suzerainty. The coin displays clear calligraphy typical of the period, though part of the legend is off the flan as is standard for these hand-struck coins.

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1151 / RY 20 (c. 1738 AD). Mint: Jahangirnagar (modern-day Dhaka, Bangladesh). Catalog: KM# 436.31.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Muhammad Shah'. The title 'Badshah' is clearly legible across the center, with 'Muhammad Shah' at the top.
Reverse: Not visible in the image, but based on the collector's holder, it contains the formula: 'Zarb Jahangirnagar Sanah 20 Julus Manus Maimanat'.

Jahangirnagar was the Mughal name for Dhaka. Coins from this mint are highly sought after by collectors of the Bengal region. The handwritten details on the holder (AH 1151, RY 20) are consistent with the historical reign of Muhammad Shah 'Rangeela'.

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1133 / RY 3 (approx. 1721 AD). Mint: Gwalior. Catalog: KM# 436.25.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Muhammad Shah' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Muhammad Shah).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Zarb Gwalior' (Associated with the prosperity of the 3rd regnal year, struck at Gwalior).

The coin is a silver rupee of the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah, often referred to as 'Rangeela'. The reverse side is clearly visible in the image, showing the regnal year '3' (Julus) and the mint name 'Gwalior' at the bottom. The information handwritten on the flip matches the visible details of the coin.

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah, Mughal Emperor
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah, Mughal Emperor. Period: AH 1131 / 1719 AD (Regnal Year: Ahad / Year 1). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM #436.60.
notes: Obverse: Standard Persian legend for Muhammad Shah: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Muhammad Shah' (The auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Muhammad Shah) along with the AH date (not visible in this image side).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah Ahad Julus Zarb Surat' which translates to 'Struck at Surat in the first year of the accession associated with prosperity'.

The coin is a silver Rupee from the first regnal year (Ahad) of Muhammad Shah, often referred to as Muhammad Shah 'Rangeela'. The flip details provided in the image match the visible Persian script on the reverse of the coin. Surat was a major maritime trade hub and a prolific mint for the Mughals.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1132 / RY 2 (c. 1720 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 436.60.
notes: Obverse: Though not visible in this image, the standard obverse features the Persian legend: "Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Muhammad Shah" (Auspicious coin of the Emperor Muhammad Shah, fighter for the faith).
Reverse: Persian legend: "Manus Maimanat Manus Zarb Surat Sanah 2 Julus" which translates to: "Associated with prosperity of the reign, struck at Surat in the 2nd regnal year."

Muhammad Shah, known as 'Rangila', had a long reign during which the Mughal Empire began to decline significantly. Coins from the Surat mint are quite common but highly collectible due to their bold calligraphy. This specific coin shows the 2nd regnal year (RY 2) clearly on the reverse.

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1134 / Regnal Year 3 (1721-1722 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM# 436.60.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Sahiba-i-Qiran Thani Muhammad Shah Badshah Ghazi' (Lucky coin of the second Lord of the auspicious Conjunction, Muhammad Shah, Emperor, Warrior against Infidels). The central part 'Muhammad Shah Badshah' is clearly visible.

Reverse: Likely contains the formula: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Julus' (In the 3rd year of his reign associated with prosperity) and the mint name 'Surat' at the bottom (as per the holder's description).

A standard silver rupee of the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah (reigned 1719-1748). The handwritten details on the coin holder (2x2 flip) correctly identify the ruler, year, and mint. Surat was a prolific mint for the Mughal Empire during this period.

items.title: Rupee - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1151 / RY 20 (1738 AD). Mint: Surat. Catalog: KM #436.60.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Muhammad Shah' with the Hijri date 1151 clearly visible at the bottom.
Reverse: Persian legend including 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 20 Julus Zarab Surat' (standard for this issue; only partially visible or on the other side).

This is a silver rupee from the reign of the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah (nicknamed 'Rangila'). The Hijri date 1151 (١١٥١) is clearly struck at the bottom of the obverse. The regnal year (Julus) 20 and the Surat mint attribution provided on the coin holder are consistent with the known types for this date. Surat was one of the most important and prolific mints of the later Mughal period.

items.title: Fractional Paisa / Dam - Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Fractional Paisa / Dam issued by Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor). Period: 1748 AD (AH 1161). Mint: Mint name off-flan (likely Shahjahanabad or Surat). Catalog: Krause KM# 430 (general copper series).
notes: Obverse: Though not clearly visible in this single-sided view, it would typically contain the Emperor's name and titles: 'Sikka Mubarak Sahib Qiran Sani Muhammad Shah Badshah Ghazi'.

Reverse: The visible side displays the Persian legend 'Sanat [RY] Manus Maimanat Zarb', which translates to 'Struck in the [Regnal Year] of prosperous association'. The word 'Zarb' is partially visible at the bottom.

This coin is a small copper fractional unit from the late Mughal period. The year written on the holder (AH 1161) corresponds to the final year of Muhammad Shah's reign. In this era, small copper coins (often called Falus or Dams) were minted in large quantities for local circulation, and identifying specific mints often requires seeing the very bottom portion of the reverse legend, which is frequently off-flan on these small types.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Akbar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Akbar (Mughal Emperor). Period: c. 1584-1605 AD (Ilahi Era). Mint: Ahmedabad. Catalog: KM #93.2.
notes: Obverse: Legend in Persian script: 'Allahu Akbar Jalla Jalaluhu' (God is Great, may His glory be glorified).
Reverse: Legend in Persian script: Mint name 'Ahmedabad', Ilahi month 'Aban' and the Regnal Year.

This is a 'Jalla Jalaluhu' type round silver rupee from the Ahmedabad mint. The coin identifies the month of issue as 'Aban' from the Ilahi calendar, which Akbar introduced in the later part of his reign (starting around 1584 AD). The small holes visible on the surface are 'shroff marks' (test marks) made by contemporary money changers to verify the silver's purity.

items.title: 1 Silver Rupee - Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: 1 Silver Rupee issued by Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar (Mughal Emperor). Period: c. 1584-1605 AD (Ilahi Era). Mint: Ahmedabad. Catalog: KM #93.2.
notes: Obverse: The obverse features the Persian legend 'Allahu Akbar Jalla Jallalahu' (God is Great, Eminent is His Glory). This is the standard legend for Akbar's Ilahi coinage.
Reverse: The reverse displays the mint name 'Ahmedabad' along with the Ilahi month 'Azar' (the 9th month of the Persian solar calendar) and the Ilahi regnal year.

This coin belongs to the 'Ilahi' series, which Akbar introduced in the 30th year of his reign to replace the Islamic Hijri calendar with a solar one. The flip (holder) provides accurate identification of the ruler, mint, and month. These coins are popular among collectors of Mughal numismatics for their artistic calligraphy and historical significance regarding Akbar's religious reforms.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1168 / RY 1 (1754 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Alamgir' with AH date 1168 (partially visible on the other side).
Reverse: Persian legend 'Manus Maimanat Sanat Julus' with mint name 'Azimabad' and Regnal Year (RY) 1 visible at the top/bottom.

The coin is a silver rupee of the Mughal Emperor Alamgir II, minted at the Azimabad (modern-day Patna) mint. The handwriting on the coin holder accurately identifies the ruler, the Hijri year 1168, and the Regnal Year 1. Azimabad was a key mint city in Bihar during the later Mughal period.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1169 / RY 2 (1755 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend containing the name and titles of the Emperor: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Aziz-ud-din Muhammad Alamgir', along with the Hijri date 1169 (not visible in this side-only image).
Reverse: Persian legend 'Manus Maimanat Manus' with the word 'Julus' (enthronement) in the center, regnal year '2' above, and the mint name 'Azimabad' clearly visible at the bottom.

Azimabad was the Mughal name for the city of Patna. This coin was minted during the short and turbulent reign of Alamgir II. The identification is highly reliable as the visible reverse side perfectly matches the handwritten attribution on the coin holder.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1169 / RY 3 (1755-1756 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Aziz-ud-din Muhammad Alamgir' (Name and titles of the ruler).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 3 Zarb Azimabad' (Struck at Azimabad in the 3rd year of his reign). The image shows this side clearly with 'Sanah 3' visible.

The identification is based on the visible Persian calligraphy which matches the handwritten attribution on the coin flip. Azimabad was a major Mughal mint in Bihar. Alamgir II's reign (1754–1759) saw the further disintegration of the Mughal Empire and the rise of British influence in Bengal/Bihar.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1170 / RY 3 (approx. 1756-1757 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM #460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Alamgir' (The auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Alamgir). The Hijri date 1170 is typically found on this side.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Azimabad Sanah 3 Julus Manus Maimanat' (Struck at Azimabad in the 3rd year of the associated prosperity).

The coin is accurately labeled on its flip. Azimabad was a major mint in the province of Bihar. Alamgir II was the 14th Mughal Emperor, and his reign was marked by the decline of central Mughal authority and the rise of regional powers like the British East India Company in Bengal/Bihar.

items.title: Rupee - Aziz-ud-din Alamgir II
items.description: Rupee issued by Aziz-ud-din Alamgir II. Period: AH 1170 / RY 4 (c. 1757 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: Krause KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Alamgir' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Alamgir)
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Azimabad Sanah 4 Julus Manus Maimanat' (Struck at Azimabad in the 4th year of his prosperous reign)

Alamgir II was the Mughal Emperor of India from 1754 to 1759. Azimabad was the Mughal name for the city of Patna. The coin holder accurately reflects the ruler, mint, and dates as per standard numismatic catalogs. This specific issue corresponds to the transition period in Bengal/Bihar politics during the rise of the British East India Company's influence.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1171 / Regnal Year 5 (approx. 1757-1758 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: The obverse (not shown, but known for this type) contains the Persian legend: 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Aziz-ud-din Muhammad Alamgir'.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanah 5 Julus Zarb Azimabad'. The numeral '5' representing the regnal year is clearly visible in the center.

Alamgir II was the 14th Mughal Emperor. Azimabad was the prestigious Mughal name for the city of Patna. The coin holder's handwritten notes accurately identify the ruler, the regnal year (Julus 5), the AH date (1171), and the mint.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1172 / Regnal Year 6 (c. 1758-1759 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Typically contains the legend 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Alamgir' (not visible in this single-sided view, but standard for the type).
Reverse: Contains the 'Manus Maimanat' formula and 'Julus' (reign) year 6. The mint name 'Azimabad' is clearly visible at the bottom.

This coin was minted during the reign of the Mughal Emperor Alamgir II. The handwritten details on the flip accurately identify the coin's origins. Azimabad (modern Patna) was a major provincial mint. Only one side of the coin (the reverse) is visible in the photograph.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)
items.description: Rupee issued by Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1172 / RY 6 (c. 1759 AD). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM# 460.5.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend (partially visible or inferred): 'Sikka Mubarak Badshah Ghazi Alamgir' (Auspicious coin of the Victorious Emperor Alamgir).
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Azimabad Sanah 6 Julus Manus Maimanat' (Struck at Azimabad in the 6th year of his reign associated with prosperity).

The coin clearly shows the reverse side with the mint name 'Azimabad' at the bottom and the regnal year '6' (۶) above the 'J' of Julus. The handwritten notes on the flip correctly identify the ruler, year, and mint. Alamgir II's coinage from Azimabad is sought after by collectors of the Mughal series.

items.title: 1 Chuckram - Princely State of Travancore
items.description: 1 Chuckram issued by Princely State of Travancore. Period: Late 19th to early 20th Century (c. 1860-1910 AD). Mint: Trivandrum. Catalog: KM# 29 / KM# 43.
notes: Obverse: Features a stylized conch shell (Shankha) facing right, which is the emblem of the Travancore state, though it is highly worn in this image.
Reverse: A complex floral or geometric pattern known as the Sudarsana Chakram (a sacred wheel/disc), appearing as a collection of dots and lines.

The coin is a tiny silver Chuckram from the Travancore region (modern-day Kerala). Due to its small size and the reflective nature of silver, these coins are often difficult to photograph. The visible curved lines are characteristic of the stylized conch design used during this period. The significant wear and poor image resolution make a precise date or sub-type identification difficult.

items.title: Rupee - Aziz-ud-din Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)

This coin was issued during the first year (Ahad) of Alamgir II's reign. The identification is supported by the handwritten label on the coin holder. Shahjahanabad was the primary mint in Delhi during the late Mughal period.

items.title: Rupee - Alamgir II (Mughal Emperor)

Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Shahjahanabad Sanah 5 Julus Manus Maimanat', which translates to 'Struck at Shahjahanabad in the 5th year of his prosperous reign'. The word 'Julus' is prominent in the center.

The identification is heavily supported by the handwritten details on the coin holder, which accurately match the visible Persian script on the coin's reverse. Alamgir II was the son of Jahandar Shah and reigned during a period of declining Mughal power.

items.description: Paisa / Falus issued by Muhammad Akbar II. Period: AH 1221 / 1806 AD (Regnal Year 1). Mint: Shahjahanabad (Delhi). Catalog: Krause KM# 621.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: Sikka Mubarak Sahib Qiran Sani Muhammad Akbar Shah (Auspicious coin of the second Lord of the Conjunctions, Muhammad Akbar Shah)
Reverse: Persian legend: Sanah ahad julus manus maimanat zarb Shahjahanabad (Struck at Shahjahanabad in the first year of the prosperous reign)

The coin belongs to the first regnal year of Muhammad Akbar II. By this period, the Mughal Emperors were largely figureheads under British protection. The labeling on the coin holder matches the characteristic Persian script visible on the coin's surface.

items.title: Silver Rupee - Shah Jahan III (Muhi-ul-Millat)
items.description: Silver Rupee issued by Shah Jahan III (Muhi-ul-Millat). Period: 1760 AD (AH 1174). Mint: Azimabad (Patna). Catalog: KM #475.1.

Reverse: Standard reverse for this issue contains the mint name 'Azimabad' and the regnal year (Julus) 'Ahad' (Year 1).

Shah Jahan III was a puppet emperor who reigned for less than a year (roughly 10 months) between 1759 and 1760. Because of his very short reign, his coins are significantly scarcer than those of major Mughal emperors.

This coin was issued during the later Mughal period. Although the holder mentions '1 Julus', the date 1248 AH corresponds to the 27th regnal year of Akbar II. The mint at Surat at this time was often operated under the influence or direct control of the British East India Company.

items.description: Gold Mohur issued by Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor). Period: AH 1175 / RY 2 (1761 AD). Mint: Muhammadabad Banaras. Catalog: KM# 110.
notes: Obverse: Standard obverse for this type bears the Persian legend 'Sikka Mubarak Sahab Qiran Sani Shah Alam Badshah', signifying the auspicious coinage of the Emperor.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Manus Maimanat Sanat 2 Julus Zarb Muhammadabad Banaras'. This translates to 'Struck at Muhammadabad Banaras in the 2nd year of the reign associated with prosperity'.

The handwritten notes on the flip accurately identify the coin. The Muhammadabad Banaras mint was very active during this period, often under the local authority of the Raja of Banaras but maintaining the Mughal imperial titulature. This specimen is in excellent condition with sharp legends and a full mint name visible on the bottom of the reverse.

items.title: 1 Rupee - Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor, issued under British East India Company administration)
items.description: 1 Rupee issued by Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor, issued under British East India Company administration). Period: AH 1218 / Ry 37 (c. 1803 AD). Mint: Bareilly (Bareli). Catalog: KM #52.
notes: Obverse: Persian legend: 'Sikka zad bar haft kishwar saya fazl elah hami din muhammad shah alam badshah' (Shadow of the divine favor, defender of the faith, Shah Alam, the king, struck coin in the seven climes). The Hijri date '١٢١٨' (1218) is visible at the bottom.
Reverse: Persian legend: 'Zarb Bareli Sanah 37 Julus Maimanat Manus' (Struck at Bareilly in the 37th year of his prosperous reign).

This coin represents the transition period where the British East India Company struck coins in the name of the Mughal Emperor. Bareilly was ceded to the British in 1801, and coins like this AH 1218 issue are sought after for their historical significance. The AH date and regnal year (Ry 37) are clearly indicated on the holder and partially visible on the coin's calligraphy.
